title: edit_file does not validate old_string equals new_string before doing pointless write
description:
## Severity
Trivial

## Dimension
Functional Correctness / KISS

## Location
src/tools/file/edit.rs:84-95 (occurrence check then replacen)

## Violation
If `old_string == new_string`, the code happily reads the file, finds the string, performs `content.replacen(old, new, 1)` (a no-op), and writes the file back unchanged. The test edit_old_equals_new_succeeds_if_present (line 217) pins this as "OC does NOT special-case this — it succeeds (no-op write) when the string exists once" while CC errors with errorCode 1.

This burns IO on a no-op AND updates the mtime, which can churn file watchers, retrigger build pipelines, and falsely appear as a modification in git. The model also receives "Successfully edited" feedback for an edit that did nothing — wasting a turn.

## Mandated refactor
At the top of execute_edit_file (right after extracting old_string and new_string but before reading the file), insert:
```rust
if old_string == new_string {
    return ("old_string and new_string are identical — edit would be a no-op. Either change one or remove the call.".to_string(), true);
}
```

## Filed from QA review dispatch
